**Gary John Megson** (born 2 May 1959)[[2]](#cite_note-2) is an English former [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") player and manager.

He has previously managed [Norwich City](/wiki/Norwich_City_F.C. "Norwich City F.C."), [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C."), [Stockport County](/wiki/Stockport_County_F.C. "Stockport County F.C."), [Stoke City](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C."), [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C."), [Nottingham Forest](/wiki/Nottingham_Forest_F.C. "Nottingham Forest F.C."), [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C."), [Bolton Wanderers](/wiki/Bolton_Wanderers_F.C. "Bolton Wanderers F.C.") and [Sheffield Wednesday](/wiki/Sheffield_Wednesday_F.C. "Sheffield Wednesday F.C."). He guided West Brom to promotion in 2001–02 and 2003–04, both times from the [First Division](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division") to the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League").

He is the son of [Don Megson](/wiki/Don_Megson "Don Megson") and the brother of [Neil Megson](/wiki/Neil_Megson_(soccer) "Neil Megson (soccer)"), both former players.

## Playing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gary_Megson&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Playing career")]

As a player, Megson was a tough-tackling [defensive midfielder](/wiki/Midfielder#Defensive_midfielder "Midfielder") who played for nine different clubs. He began his career at [Plymouth Argyle](/wiki/Plymouth_Argyle_F.C. "Plymouth Argyle F.C."), where he impressed enough for [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") to sign him for a £250,000 transfer fee. Megson struggled to establish himself in the Everton line-up, and after two years at Goodison, he moved to [Sheffield Wednesday](/wiki/Sheffield_Wednesday_F.C. "Sheffield Wednesday F.C."), where his father had once played, for a fee of £130,000.

Megson immediately gained a place in Wednesday's starting lineup, and was a member of the team that gained promotion to the top flight in [1983–84](/wiki/1983%E2%80%9384_in_English_football "1983–84 in English football"), ending a 14-year exile from the elite. In his three years at [Hillsborough](/wiki/Hillsborough_Stadium "Hillsborough Stadium"), he missed only three league games. In the summer of 1984, he was signed by [Nottingham Forest](/wiki/Nottingham_Forest_F.C. "Nottingham Forest F.C."), only for [Brian Clough](/wiki/Brian_Clough "Brian Clough") to decide "he couldn't trap a bag of cement". Megson spent five months at the City Ground, without making a single first-team appearance before being sold to [Newcastle United](/wiki/Newcastle_United_F.C. "Newcastle United F.C.").

Megson played regularly for the Magpies for the remainder of the [1984–85 season](/wiki/1984%E2%80%9385_in_English_football "1984–85 in English football"), but lost his place in the line-up the following season, and moved back to Sheffield Wednesday. In his second spell with the Owls, Megson again established himself as an important member of the squad, and was rarely out of the starting eleven. In January 1989, he moved to [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C."), where he spent three and a half seasons, and helped City finish fifth in his final two seasons there, having helped them win promotion in his first season.

He then moved to [Norwich City](/wiki/Norwich_City_F.C. "Norwich City F.C.") on a free transfer in the summer of 1992, and spent three seasons at Carrow Road. He was an important member of the Norwich side that finished third in the inaugural season of the Premier League and played in the UEFA Cup for the first time as a result. When manager [Mike Walker](/wiki/Mike_Walker_(Welsh_footballer) "Mike Walker (Welsh footballer)") moved to [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") in January 1994, he was also assistant to the new Norwich manager [John Deehan](/wiki/John_Deehan "John Deehan").

When Deehan resigned in April 1995, Megson briefly took charge as caretaker manager, but failed to save City from the drop, losing four and drawing one of his five games in charge. In the summer, he also left Norwich and finished his playing career with short spells at lower division sides [Lincoln City](/wiki/Lincoln_City_F.C. "Lincoln City F.C.") and [Shrewsbury Town](/wiki/Shrewsbury_Town_F.C. "Shrewsbury Town F.C."). In December 1995, he returned to Norwich when he was re-appointed manager following [Martin O'Neill](/wiki/Martin_O%27Neill "Martin O'Neill")'s sudden departure to [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C."). He remained as manager at [Carrow Road](/wiki/Carrow_Road "Carrow Road") until the end of the season, when Mike Walker was appointed for the second time.

## Managerial career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gary_Megson&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Managerial career")]

### Norwich City

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gary_Megson&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Norwich City")]

While still playing at Norwich City, Megson became assistant to manager [John Deehan](/wiki/John_Deehan "John Deehan"). When Deehan resigned as manager of Norwich on 9 April 1995, with Norwich heading for relegation after a terrible loss of form since Christmas, Megson stepped up to the manager's seat for the remainder of the [season](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_in_English_football "1994–95 in English football"), and had five games remaining to secure survival. However, Norwich collected just one point from their remaining games and were relegated. [Martin O'Neill](/wiki/Martin_O%27Neill "Martin O'Neill") was appointed manager that summer, with Megson leaving the club to play for Lincoln City and then Shrewsbury, before joining [Bradford City](/wiki/Bradford_City "Bradford City") late in 1995 as assistant manager to [Chris Kamara](/wiki/Chris_Kamara "Chris Kamara"). However, by Christmas that year, he was back at Norwich as manager following the departure of Martin O'Neill to Leicester City. Norwich finished 15th in Division One that season and Megson then left the club as [Mike Walker](/wiki/Mike_Walker_(Welsh_footballer) "Mike Walker (Welsh footballer)") returned as manager.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

### Blackpool

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gary_Megson&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Blackpool")]

In 1996, Megson became manager at [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C."), where he recorded 21 wins in 52 matches – enough to stay clear of relegation to [Division Three](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division "Football League Third Division"), but not quite enough for a playoff place and the chance of promotion to the [Division One](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division"). At [Bloomfield Road](/wiki/Bloomfield_Road "Bloomfield Road"), he was assisted by the former [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") midfielder [Mike Phelan](/wiki/Mike_Phelan "Mike Phelan"), but the partnership failed to bring a Division Two playoff place to *the Seasiders*, and Megson left at the end of the season.

### Stockport County

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gary_Megson&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Stockport County")]

Megson moved to [Stockport County](/wiki/Stockport_County_F.C. "Stockport County F.C.") in July 1997, shortly after [Dave Jones](/wiki/Dave_Jones_(football_manager) "Dave Jones (football manager)")' departure for [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton_F.C. "Southampton F.C."). County had been runners-up in [Division Two](/wiki/EFL_League_One "EFL League One") the season before, therefore started the new season in [Division One](/wiki/EFL_Championship "EFL Championship"). In Megson's first season they performed admirably and they came just two places short of the Division One playoffs, with some excellent performances, including a 3–1 home win versus [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C."). The following season was more disappointing and County finished in 16th place. Soon after he left Stockport by mutual consent.

### Stoke City

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gary_Megson&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Stoke City")]

Megson was not unemployed for long, taking the manager's job at [Stoke City](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C.") in the summer of 1999 after the club missed out on [Tony Pulis](/wiki/Tony_Pulis "Tony Pulis") who instead joined [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C.").[[4]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-4) Megson arrived at the club at a time when there was a great deal of pressure on the current board to sell the club to new owners.[[4]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-4) After a slow start to the [1999–2000 season](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_Stoke_City_F.C._season "1999–2000 Stoke City F.C. season") results gradually improved and the team went on a ten match unbeaten run to haul them into the play-offs. However unfortunately for Megson by November the long-awaited takeover bid was accepted in the form of a group of Icelandic businessmen headed by [Gunnar Gíslason](/wiki/Gunnar_G%C3%ADslason_(businessman) "Gunnar Gíslason (businessman)").[[4]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-4) They appointed their own man, former [Iceland national team](/wiki/Iceland_national_football_team "Iceland national football team") manager [Gudjon Thordarson](/wiki/Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3n_%C3%9E%C3%B3r%C3%B0arson "Guðjón Þórðarson").[[4]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-4)

### West Bromwich Albion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gary_Megson&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: West Bromwich Albion")]

Megson took over as manager of [First Division](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division") [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") in March 2000, just days before the [transfer deadline](/wiki/Transfer_deadline "Transfer deadline") at the end of the [1999–2000 season](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_in_English_football "1999–2000 in English football"). It was 14 years since Albion had been in the top flight of [English football](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system"), and in that time they had spent their first ever spell (two seasons) in the third tier. Indeed, when Megson was appointed they were in real danger of a second relegation to that level. But he moved quickly to strengthen the team by bringing in several new players. In his first month in charge, Megson received a 28-day touchline ban following his comments to referee [Graham Poll](/wiki/Graham_Poll "Graham Poll") after Albion's 2–0 defeat to [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth_F.C. "Portsmouth F.C."). Megson was unhappy about a penalty that Poll had awarded to Portsmouth; some years later, Poll conceded that it was "one of the worst penalties I've ever given".[[5]](#cite_note-5) Albion nevertheless ensured their safety by winning their final game of the season.

The following season Megson took the club into the playoffs, winning the [Division One Manager of the Month](/wiki/Football_League_Championship_Manager_of_the_Month "Football League Championship Manager of the Month") award for November 2000 along the way.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Albion lost to Bolton Wanderers at the [semi-final](/wiki/Semi-final "Semi-final") stage, but [the following year](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football "2001–02 in English football") the club won promotion to the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") for the first time, overcoming the eleven-point lead of their fierce local rivals [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.") in the closing weeks of the campaign. This achievement earned Megson the Nationwide Division One Manager of the Year award,[[7]](#cite_note-7) as well as the medieval title *Lord of the Manor of West Bromwich*.[[8]](#cite_note-8) However, the club was barely prepared for the financial challenges of life in the top flight and a bitter quarrel soon developed between Megson and the club's chairman Paul Thompson over what Megson perceived as the latter's interference in footballing matters. An undignified public showdown resulted in Thompson resigning from the board in order to forestall Megson's departure. The board elected [Jeremy Peace](/wiki/Jeremy_Peace "Jeremy Peace") as Thompson's successor, and in July 2002 Megson signed a new three-year contract with Albion.[[9]](#cite_note-9) However, in 2002–03 the club were relegated after just a single season in the Premier League.

Megson mounted a successful promotion campaign [the following season](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_in_English_football "2003–04 in English football"), and a return to the Premier League – but by the summer of 2004, the relationship between Megson and Peace had become strained. By September, after a poor start to the season, Megson's job appeared to be under threat. The following month Megson, whose contract was due to end in June 2005, announced that he would not sign a new deal if the club offered one. The board chose to interpret this as a resignation, and on 26 October Megson was dismissed.[[10]](#cite_note-10) A settlement for the remainder of his contract was reached in November 2004.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Nottingham Forest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gary_Megson&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Nottingham Forest")]

Within a week of being sacked at West Bromwich Albion, Gary Megson was linked with the manager's job at Albion's local rivals [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C."), which became vacant after [Dave Jones](/wiki/Dave_Jones_(football_manager) "Dave Jones (football manager)") was sacked – but that job went to [Glenn Hoddle](/wiki/Glenn_Hoddle "Glenn Hoddle") instead. On 10 January 2005, Megson was appointed to succeed [Joe Kinnear](/wiki/Joe_Kinnear "Joe Kinnear") as manager of struggling [Nottingham Forest](/wiki/Nottingham_Forest_F.C. "Nottingham Forest F.C."),[[12]](#cite_note-12) but was unable to save them from slipping into the third tier of English football for the first time in more than half a century. Promotion back to the [Championship](/wiki/EFL_Championship "EFL Championship") was to be his priority for [2005–06](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_in_English_football "2005–06 in English football"), but Forest struggled, especially away from home, for most of the season. In terms of points they were nearer to the relegation zone than the playoff zone when he resigned in February 2006 after being put under a lot of pressure by the Forest fans.[[13]](#cite_note-13) He claimed that his resignation had cost him £500,000.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In June 2007 Megson was appointed as a coach at [Stoke City](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C.") by manager [Tony Pulis](/wiki/Tony_Pulis "Tony Pulis"). He retained this role until his appointment as manager of Leicester City.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Leicester City

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gary_Megson&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Leicester City")]

On 13 September 2007, [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.") chairman [Milan Mandarić](/wiki/Milan_Mandari%C4%87 "Milan Mandarić") announced Gary Megson as the new manager of the club, citing Megson's "wealth of experience" as a deciding factor in the appointment. Leicester achieved their first League win under Megson on 6 October 2007 with a 2–0 win over [Sheffield Wednesday](/wiki/Sheffield_Wednesday_F.C. "Sheffield Wednesday F.C.") at [Hillsborough](/wiki/Hillsborough_Stadium "Hillsborough Stadium") with goals from [Gareth McAuley](/wiki/Gareth_McAuley "Gareth McAuley") and an own goal by [Akpo Sodje](/wiki/Akpo_Sodje "Akpo Sodje").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

A month into Megson's tenure at Leicester, Mandarić rejected an approach from [Bolton Wanderers](/wiki/Bolton_Wanderers_F.C. "Bolton Wanderers F.C.") for the manager's services.[[17]](#cite_note-Foxes_reject_Bolton's_Megson_move-17) On 23 October however, Bolton announced that Megson was their first choice to become their new manager, and made a second approach for him. The club said they were also willing to compensate Leicester should Megson leave the [Walkers Stadium](/wiki/Walkers_Stadium "Walkers Stadium").[[18]](#cite_note-18) This second approach was also rejected by Milan Mandarić,[[19]](#cite_note-19) but Megson was eventually given permission to speak to Bolton and he left Leicester on 24 October 2007, just 41 days and nine [EFL Championship](/wiki/EFL_Championship "EFL Championship") games after his appointment.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Bolton Wanderers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gary_Megson&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Bolton Wanderers")]

Megson took over as manager of [Bolton Wanderers](/wiki/Bolton_Wanderers_F.C. "Bolton Wanderers F.C.") on 25 October 2007 in a two-and-a-half-year deal.[[21]](#cite_note-21) He accepted that he was not the number one choice for the job,[[22]](#cite_note-22) after Bolton had already had approaches for [Steve Bruce](/wiki/Steve_Bruce "Steve Bruce") and [Chris Coleman](/wiki/Chris_Coleman_(footballer) "Chris Coleman (footballer)") rejected,[[17]](#cite_note-Foxes_reject_Bolton's_Megson_move-17) and [Graeme Souness](/wiki/Graeme_Souness "Graeme Souness") had also ruled himself out.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Bolton had made a poor start to the [2007–08 season](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_in_English_football "2007–08 in English football") under [Sammy Lee](/wiki/Sammy_Lee_(footballer) "Sammy Lee (footballer)"), and when Megson took over they were bottom of the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") table with only 5 points from 10 games. Megson's first game in charge was against [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.") on 28 October, and finished 1–1. He recorded his first win on 24 November when Bolton beat [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C."), the champions and League leaders, 1–0; this was their first home victory over United for 30 years.

By the [New Year](/wiki/New_Year "New Year") Bolton were lying 16th in the Premier League table, but only two points ahead of [Fulham](/wiki/Fulham_F.C. "Fulham F.C.") who were 19th. In January 2008 they sold [Nicolas Anelka](/wiki/Nicolas_Anelka "Nicolas Anelka") to [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") for £15 million, and brought in no comparable replacement. In February 2008 however they beat [Atlético Madrid](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Madrid "Atlético Madrid") (who at the time were lying fourth in [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga")) 1–0 on [aggregate](/wiki/Aggregate_score "Aggregate score"), winning 1–0 at home and drawing 0–0 away, to reach the last 16 of the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup") for the first time in the club's history before proceeding to play the reserve team in Lisbon in the Last 16 with the tie tied at 1–1, Bolton lost the away leg 1–0. Megson picked a reserve side so that the first team were rested for Sunday's relegation battle against [Wigan Athletic](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_F.C. "Wigan Athletic F.C."). They proceeded to lose 1–0 to a ten-man Wigan side. Their League form remained poor, and a 4–0 defeat at [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.") on 5 April left them in 18th place, two points adrift of safety. But they proceeded to take 11 points from their last five games (including a 1–1 draw at Chelsea on the final day of the season) to secure survival in the Premier League – the first time Megson had achieved this as a manager.

Megson won the Premier League Manager of the Month award for November 2008.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Heading into the 2008–09 season, he brought in players such as [Johan Elmander](/wiki/Johan_Elmander "Johan Elmander") for a club record £8.2m, [Fabrice Muamba](/wiki/Fabrice_Muamba "Fabrice Muamba") from [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City "Birmingham City") for £5m, [Mustapha Riga](/wiki/Mustapha_Riga "Mustapha Riga") from [Levante](/wiki/Levante_UD "Levante UD"), [Danny Shittu](/wiki/Danny_Shittu "Danny Shittu") for £2m and [Ebi Smolarek](/wiki/Ebi_Smolarek "Ebi Smolarek") on a season-long loan from [Racing Santander](/wiki/Racing_Santander "Racing Santander") with a view to a permanent deal. Bolton started the season unremarkably and by October Bolton were looking set for another relegation scrap. This caused increasing pressure on Megson but he was given time by Chairman [Phil Gartside](/wiki/Phil_Gartside "Phil Gartside") and managed to improve results, leading Bolton to a high of eighth in the league. On 1 March 2009, it was announced that Megson had agreed a new rolling contract with Bolton.[[25]](#cite_note-25) He celebrated his new contract with a 1–0 victory over [Newcastle United](/wiki/Newcastle_United "Newcastle United").[[26]](#cite_note-26) Bolton finished 13th in the 2008–09 season.

During the summer of 2009 Megson added to the Trotters' squad with the additions of [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth") midfielder [Sean Davis](/wiki/Sean_Davis_(footballer,_born_1979) "Sean Davis (footballer, born 1979)") on a free transfer, [Hull City](/wiki/Hull_City_A.F.C. "Hull City A.F.C.")'s Welsh right-back [Sam Ricketts](/wiki/Sam_Ricketts "Sam Ricketts"), [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa "Aston Villa")'s England international defender [Zat Knight](/wiki/Zat_Knight "Zat Knight") for £4m, veteran left-back [Paul Robinson](/wiki/Paul_Robinson_(defender_born_1978) "Paul Robinson (defender born 1978)") on loan from [West Brom](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") and the South Korean international [Lee Chung-Yong](/wiki/Lee_Chung-Yong "Lee Chung-Yong"). Just before the transfer window closed he added the Croatian international [Ivan Klasnić](/wiki/Ivan_Klasni%C4%87 "Ivan Klasnić") from [FC Nantes](/wiki/FC_Nantes "FC Nantes") on a season long loan. In the 2009 season, under Megson's tenure, he started the season in an inauspicious manner with defeats to Hull City and Sunderland before seeing excellent form in the next seven games. These games saw three wins, two draws and narrow defeats to Manchester United and Liverpool. After 25 October, Bolton lost five out of the next six games. The first three were losses by four goals and these were followed by a home defeat to Blackburn, a draw against Fulham and an away defeat at Wolverhampton Wanderers. In a period which lasted almost fifty days without a win Bolton played Manchester City and were scheduled games against other relegation candidates including West Ham United, Wigan Athletic, Burnley and Hull City. In the four games that were played Bolton were leading them all but ended with only one win and three draws, leaving the club 18th out of 20 and with 18 points from 18 games.

On 30 December 2009, Bolton announced that Megson had been relieved of his duties as manager with immediate effect. Bolton Wanderers official club statement said; "The decision has been taken in the light of the position the club finds itself in the Barclays Premier League at the halfway point of the season."[[27]](#cite_note-27) It was later confirmed that as the club and their former manager could not agree a compensation deal Megson would be paid out the remainder of his rolling contract on a weekly basis, effectively putting him on a year's [garden leave](/wiki/Garden_leave "Garden leave").[[28]](#cite_note-28) Over two years after being dismissed by Bolton, Megson complained that he had never won the fans over and hit out at the club's supporters, claiming that they did not like him and he did not like them.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Sheffield Wednesday

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gary_Megson&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Sheffield Wednesday")]

On 4 February 2011, Megson was appointed manager of his former club, [Sheffield Wednesday](/wiki/Sheffield_Wednesday_F.C. "Sheffield Wednesday F.C."), replacing [Alan Irvine](/wiki/Alan_Irvine_(footballer_born_1958) "Alan Irvine (footballer born 1958)") who had been relieved of his post the previous day. On 29 February 2012, with the club third in League One, he was sacked. At the time of his sacking he had the third best win percentage of any manager in Sheffield Wednesday's history. His last result before being sacked was a 1–0 victory over [Sheffield United](/wiki/Sheffield_United_F.C. "Sheffield United F.C.") in the [Steel City derby](/wiki/Steel_City_derby "Steel City derby").[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Return to West Bromwich Albion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gary_Megson&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Return to West Bromwich Albion")]

Megson returned to West Bromwich Albion as an Assistant Head Coach to [Tony Pulis](/wiki/Tony_Pulis "Tony Pulis") in July 2017.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

On 20 November 2017, he was named as the caretaker head coach of [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") "until further notice" after [Tony Pulis](/wiki/Tony_Pulis "Tony Pulis") was sacked.[[32]](#cite_note-32) When [Alan Pardew](/wiki/Alan_Pardew "Alan Pardew") was appointed manager of the club, Megson departed.

## Coaching style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gary_Megson&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Coaching style")]

[Darren Huckerby](/wiki/Darren_Huckerby "Darren Huckerby") criticised Megson's coaching style, when explaining why he chose to sign for Norwich City, instead of Megson's West Bromwich Albion. "I told him I didn't like the way he coached, I didn't like the way he shouted at his players and didn't like the way he treated seasoned professionals like 15-year-olds. I was just being honest with him. I said: 'I've seen you on the sidelines and you look like a crazed animal'".[[33]](#cite_note-Huck-33) According to Huckerby, Megson's response was that "He told me that was how he had to work with his players".[[33]](#cite_note-Huck-33)
